data.title: SPPP: Not Sure About PGMs, And Certainly Not About Physical Trusts
data.description:
The Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (SPPP) (SPPP:CA) is a closed-end fund that can give investors exposure to platinum and palladium, which together effectively encompass the market price of PGMs, used primarily in the catalytic converters of cars as their majority application, primarily palladium, whereas at least platinum has some more industrial uses.

Our position on the SPPP is the following:

  • Because of exposure to PGM prices, we are cautious, as we think there are threats to the direction of PGMs due to the Middle Eastern geopolitical situation.
  • As equity researchers and value investors, we cannot countenance, frankly almost ever, buying a physical trust for a commodity over buying a business that mines that commodity, which can operationally add value and provide a productive yield, particularly when physical trusts carry high fees to keep custody over the physical assets.

On both those counts, our position is not to get involved in PGM bets, as we find the geopolitical drivers of the price currently too unpredictable and don't want to take a position one way or another due to the risks and the volatility of the commodity.

Breakdown

The physical trust has a management fee of 1.01%. This is quite normal for physical trusts and is higher than diversified exposures in the form of ETFs, which usually carry lower passive fees. Due to the need to have physical custody of a commodity, the expenses are higher. Discussing the matter of how to get exposure to PGMs, we'd immediately argue that miners, such as Valterra Platinum Limited (ANGPY), make more sense since they are a productive company, adding value operationally through asset development, and pay a dividend, albeit highly variable based effectively on PGM prices. Therefore, it provides the same exposure but also the capacity to compound value as an operating mining business, an established one at that. The performance bears out the advantage, too.

The downside of Valterra over a physical trust of the commodity is that there is the differential risk of Valterra being a South Africa-based miner. While not an immediate concern, South Africa has a higher political risk of things like asset appropriation and arbitrary changes in concession charges and regulation, for example, in labour regulation, which could independently affect Valterra but not a physical trust and the price of the underlying commodity, since it's an operational thing and not a PGM market dynamic. Still, as investors and business owners, we are much more comfortable with Valterra over a quite high management fee physical trust, particularly with Valterra not having this issue at the moment, and also achieving solid cost savings.

Then there's the discussion of the PGM market:

Bull Case

The bull case would be:

  • ICE bans are untenable in Europe economically and industrially to some extent, and have backpedaled after the Ukraine war demonstrated how much we need fossil fuels. In the US, the Trump administration has been moving against authorities that may restrict emissions further in passenger vehicles. In general, there have been downward revisions in BEV demand curves. ICEs and the catalytic converters used in them, and driving PGM demand, seem like they could be here to stay longer than expected.
  • Maybe the Iran war will properly end. The ceasefire is running into political issues, but once the energy crisis ends, there is no more imminent reason to consider reducing household dependence on ICE vehicles, and also demand destruction for leveraged purchases will cease as disinflation becomes possible once again.

Bear Case

While we are not necessarily leaning towards the case, we want to say:

  • It's still very unclear whether the war will be resolved. Iran's demands are significant and may not be acceptable. Furthermore, regardless of how the war ends, there is uncertainty whether Iran will continue to control the Strait and institute its significant tolls that put a floor on the oil price. Additionally, there is a risk of other future oil crises, perhaps again around the Strait, with new threats and anxieties in the region now having emerged. The reinflation issue is a demand destruction risk for all purchases, especially leveraged ones like cars in general.
  • Then there is also the fact that there's been anecdotal evidence of the adoption of EVs happening as a consequence of higher gas prices at the pump, at least higher inquiries at certain dealerships. It is possible that this also accelerated renewable adoption in general, though that's not what happened in the last energy crisis around Ukraine, but there are new geopolitical fault lines; for example, maybe the EU cannot count on the advantage of allied US oil as much and may want to accelerate its renewable development.

Bottom Line

The bottom line is we don't want a physical trust with its fees; we'd rather go for Valterra for our PGM exposure. The other thing is that PGM demand, or specifically platinum and palladium demand, is around 65%+ driven by catalytic converters as the end market. The view even by the miners is that a surplus is coming, maybe in 2028, but some analysts say as soon as this year, and at any rate, growth in car demand will depend a lot on the end of a war that is threatening to re-raise rates and reinflate the economy. The war may also trigger more interest in renewables and EVs that may persist in the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Byrna Technologies: Conversion Collapse Is The Real Problem, Not Demand
data.description:
Byrna Technologies Inc. (BYRN) has fallen 54% since my last report. I have a Buy rating for the manufacturer of non-lethal launchers and safety equipment, but the reality is that the company is facing substantial headwinds from its sales mix. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q1 2026 earnings and update my price target.

Byrna Fails To Translate Sales Growth To Profit Growth

During the first quarter, sales rose 11% to $29 million, missing analyst estimates by $0.7 million. Revenues rose 11%, driven by continued sales expansion in the dealer and chain store channels, partially offset by post-holiday moderation and some quarter-end backlog slipping into the next quarter. Gross profit rose 9% to $17.4 million, but this indicates a margin decrease from 61% to 60% as the lower-margin dealer and chain store channels made up a larger portion of the sales, and those margins are lower compared to the e-commerce sales channel. Operating expenses rose 16% to $16.5 million, driven by higher market costs and costs in support of the expansion of the retail distribution channel. As a result, pre-tax income declined from $1.8 million to $0.8 million.

A Weak Outlook And e-Commerce Conversion

Byrna Technologies has lost 31% of its value since announcing its earnings. The reason is two-fold. First, we note that Q1 showed that higher revenues are not translating to higher profits. Secondly, CEO Conn Davis indicated during the earnings call that Q2 sales will be materially lower:

Looking ahead to Q2, I have spent my first week on the job aligned around one clear objective, winning the fight for revenue while simultaneously building the long-term foundation. Based on what we are seeing today, fiscal Q2 is developing materially below our expectations and below both the year-over-year and sequential improvement, we would ultimately expect this business to deliver. Part of that reflects a tougher comparison against last year's fiscal second quarter, which benefited from the CL launch and initial load-in orders with new retail partners of roughly $2.7 million.

Management points at the timing of products last year, but overall conversion rates in their high-margin e-commerce channel are down. The company is attracting stable traffic, but conversion is down materially.

There are various reasons for that. Some of the sales potential is shifting from high-margin e-commerce to lower-margin retail in-store sales. So, it could be that people go to the website of Byrna, check out a product, and then go to the store to buy the product. For Byrna, that is a lower margin sale. Furthermore, the audience is shifting from gun enthusiasts and gun owners to a wider audience, but the website is not built for that, and comparing several of Byrna’s solutions against each other is difficult. That may either mean that potential customers do not buy at all or they go to the store to win information and buy in-store. Further, the company is not effectively upselling.

So, the funnel is broken at the bottom, it seems. However, we also note that changes such as leveraging AI for ad generation may have a large reach but may result in the wrong audience being attracted to the site. Furthermore, the low conversion, which basically seems to be the result of a new audience coming to a website built for gun owners and enthusiasts, may also provide an indicator that the latter audience is highly saturated. So, there is a conversion gap there.

Byrna Technologies Remains A Buy, But Management Needs To Execute Better

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The weaker outlook for Q2 is definitely disappointing, and from the earnings call, I also get the sense that management was caught off guard by the conversion gap in their high-margin business. So, they really have to streamline the site for better audience matching and conversion and also figure out the interference dynamics between the e-commerce side of the business and their retail channel. That has spared a complete rerating on my valuation window for Byrna Technologies, and at current prices there is no upside for the stock for this year, but 58% for next year. So, the very high-risk target would be around $10, implying nearly 60% upside. That is below the prior base case of $18.19. The valuation model would normally imply a strong buy rating, but I have added a manual point-based override in my rating for Byrna to account for the weaker conversion in their high-margin channel, and that would bring the rating to buy.

With a weaker second quarter and a shift in the sales distribution, I do expect that margins will dip this year. Against a lower expected CapEx figure and an improving operating cash flow reflecting inventory burn-off, there should be a meaningful uplift in the free cash flow performance. By 2027, the margins should be largely recovered, but the working capital and inventory tailwind that I expect for 2026 is not occurring again, leading to lower free cash flow expectations. The company has no bank debt, which is great, but execution is what matters now.

Conclusion: Byrna Technologies Needs To Realign And Dissolve Channel Interference

For Byrna, it seems that demand is not the problem, but the company has a major conversion headwind in positioning itself for a wide audience in the e-commerce business, which leads to low conversion and either a loss of sales or the sales shifting from the high-margin channel to lower-margin channels. That is something the company has now found out, and it needs to address that. So, the demand and upside are there for BYRN, but there also is execution risk.

data.title: The Storm Has Passed For Tilly's
data.description:
Just over a year ago, I decided to revisit Tilly's, Inc. (TLYS), a rather interesting company that focuses on selling casual apparel, footwear, accessories, and certain hard goods. In general, this is not a space that I find to be the most attractive. It is heavily competitive, and margins have a tendency to be low. But even by the standards of the space, Tilly's was facing some issues. Declining revenue and significant losses and cash outflows proved problematic. And I argued that there were better opportunities for market participants. At the end of the day, I ended up reaffirming the company as a ‘sell’ candidate. Since then, the stock has been virtually flat while the S&P 500 is up 14%. This certainly matches my expectations for a bearish rating. After all, just because I give a company a bearish rating does not mean I expect the share price to drop. Instead, it just means that I expect the stock to underperform the market for the foreseeable future.

Having said that, since I originally turned bearish about the business in April of 2023, shares have fallen drastically. They are currently down 53.1% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 50.5%. This is despite the fact that the firm has a tremendous amount of cash on hand relative to its overall market size. And it can be attributed to continued weakness from an operational standpoint. Management does expect, however, that the picture will change this year. They are actually forecasting rather meaningful revenue growth even as the number of locations that the business has in operation declines. Uncertainty still exists here. However, the picture is safe enough to justify an upgrade to a speculative ‘hold’ rating.

Tilly's is getting a little more fashionable

Over the last three years, Tilly's has faced some serious issues. From 2023 through 2025, as an example, revenue for the company dropped from $623.1 million to $553.6 million. This decline was driven by a couple of factors. After seeing comparable store sales drop by 10.6% in 2023, they fell a further 8% in 2024. We did, fortunately, see a small increase of 0.3% in 2025, so at least in that regard, we are seeing signs of stabilization. But the bigger issue for the business has been a decline in the number of locations that it has in operation. From 2023 through 2025, that number fell quite sharply from 249 to 223. Management has been working hard to shut down underperforming locations as it pushes toward profitability once again. It is worth noting that even though the number of locations in operation has dropped, the firm is seeing a bit of resilience when it comes to its e-commerce sales. They dropped only 1.8% year over year compared to the 3.1% drop that the business saw when it came to its physical stores.

We have seen a bit of progress in this regard. After seeing its net loss expand from $34.5 million in 2023 to $46.2 million in 2024, the company reported an improvement in its net loss to $17.5 million in 2025. Even though the decline in revenue would normally hurt a business like this, the firm enjoyed meaningful cost reductions. As an example, its cost of goods sold, excluding rent paid to a related party, improved from 73.06% in 2024 to 69.62% in 2025. I know that doesn't seem like much of a change. But when applied to the revenue generated in 2025, that's an extra $19 million in pre-tax savings. According to management, the company benefited from higher initial markups on its merchandise, as well as lower markdowns. More current inventory made this possible. On top of this, the firm enjoyed a decrease in buying, distribution, and occupancy costs, thanks to a net reduction in its store count of 17 locations. Meanwhile, selling, general, and administrative costs did rise in relation to revenue. But that was not enough to offset the improvements on the gross profit side of things.

Other profitability metrics are showing some signs of improvement as well. From 2024 to 2025, the firm’s operating cash flow went from negative $42 million to positive $4.1 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $28.9 million to negative $4.1 million. Finally, we did see some weakness when it came to EBITDA, with that metric contracting from $9.9 million to $5 million. So not everything is perfect across the board.

Management has not provided detailed guidance when it comes to 2026 in its entirety. But they did provide some insights into the turnaround progress of the business, as well as what they think will happen for the first quarter. They stated, for instance, that they saw a 20% comparable net sales increase year over year in February of this year. The business is continuing to benefit from certain infrastructure investments dedicated to a price optimization tool, as well as warehouse management software that has already paid off in the form of certain cost savings. Investments in an AI-driven merchandise allocation tool have also been announced. The entire goal here seems to be centered around improving merchandise assortments, investing more heavily in digital marketing, and introducing changes to the overall brand. On top of this, while the number of locations in operation will drop from 238 in the first quarter of 2025 to 220 in the first quarter of this year, management did reveal that they expect to open between four and six new locations throughout the fiscal year in its entirety.

As a result of these efforts, management expects revenue for the first quarter of 2026 to range between $119 million and $125 million. That would be between 16% and 22% above what the company saw at the same time in 2025. But outside of this, we don't really know too much of what to expect. This does make it difficult to really value the company, especially in light of the fact that financial performance has been so weak as of late.

For instance, if we were to value the company on an EV-to-EBITDA basis using 2025 figures, it would be trading at a multiple of 16.3. That is certainly overvalued for a company with this track record. However, the changes that the firm is making should improve profitability by some degree. In the chart above, I calculated how much adjusted operating cash flow the company would need to generate to be fairly valued at a price-to-adjusted operating cash flow multiple of between 5 and 10. I did the same thing for EBITDA regarding the EV-to-EBITDA multiple. These are not unrealistic numbers. For instance, using 2025 sales figures as an example, the firm would need an EBITDA margin of between 1.48% and 2.94% to hit this range. That is entirely within the realm of possibility, especially given the improvements that the company has exhibited.

To be honest with you, while analyzing the company, I came very close to reaffirming it as a ‘sell’ candidate only because I would like to see more improvement before becoming neutral. However, after seeing that comparable sales jumped 10.3% in the final quarter of 2025 while e-commerce comparable sales grew 9.8%, I became a bit more optimistic. It also helps that the firm has no debt on its books and enjoys a cash position of $46.3 million. For a business with a market capitalization today of $128 million, that is massive. And it certainly reduces the risk profile of the company and gives it some runway to work with.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that is available right now, it is my belief that Tilly's makes for an interesting prospect. It's not one that I am ready to turn bullish on just yet. But I do think that, given the changes that we have seen and the strength of its balance sheet, upgrading it to a speculative ‘hold’ is appropriate.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Cheap For A Reason
data.description:
It's been almost a year since our previous article on Super Micro Computer (SMCI) was released. Back then, we argued that the company's valuation was detached from the business's fundamentals. Our model back then gave Super Micro an intrinsic value of $25.47 per share at a time when its stock was trading much higher. Today, the stock is trading around $23 per share, and at first glance, it may seem that the main share price swing has already taken place.

However, if we look closely at the latest developments, we'll see that the key risks have increased and also become more structural in recent months. In addition, the relatively weak earnings results and the overall changing sentiment surrounding semiconductor stocks made us even more skeptical about Super Micro. That is why we continue to rate the company's stock as a Sell since the downside seems to be greater than we expected last year.

The Exposure to a Limited Number of Customers Is a Problem

Before diving deep into our bearish thesis, it is worth explaining the nature of growth that Super Micro has been demonstrating. At first, Super Micro really looks like one of the beneficiaries of the AI boom. Since the demand for AI-optimized servers remains high, the company has been able to quickly occupy its niche as a flexible solution integrator. As a result, its revenue has been growing at a rapid pace, and this has created the impression of a strong growth story.

But the key problem is that this growth is not stable in the long run. It depends heavily on the capital costs of a few large customers. In fact, the business is tied to the investment cycles of hyperscalers and large AI players. This means that revenue visibility remains low, while the volatility is high. When demand is shaped not by the end user but by the budgets of a few large corporations, any slowdown in investment can be reflected instantly in the results. And this is the fundamental weakness of the Super Micro business model.

Margin Issues Remain

Despite the rapid growth of revenues, the company does not show a proportional improvement in profitability. And this is not a coincidence but a consequence of its position in the value chain. Super Micro does not control key technologies and does not have its own product with high added value. It works as an integrator that collects solutions from components of other manufacturers.

Under such a model, it is very difficult to create a stable competitive advantage. Super Micro is forced to compete at the expense of price, speed, and flexibility, but not at the expense of unique technology. This means that margins remain under constant pressure, especially during periods of increased competition. The latest data only confirm this thesis. Despite the growth in volumes, operational leverage does not work as expected by the market. Margin does not expand and sometimes even decreases due to price competition and rising costs. This is a critical point that is often ignored by the bullish thesis.

The AI Infrastructure Cycle Is Not Endless

Another problem with Super Micro is that it's heavily exposed to the AI infrastructure cycle. Today, the market behaves as if this cycle of constant record capital spending will continue indefinitely. It makes sense to think that since the tech companies are investing tens of billions of dollars in data centers, the demand for servers remains extremely high. But historically, such phases always end the same way. First, there is an aggressive increase in capacities. Then there is a saturation phase. Finally, there's a normalization or even a decline in investments.

It can already be seen that the scale of capital expenditure has reached levels that are difficult to maintain in the long term. For Super Micro, this means direct risk. Its business has no protection in the form of recurring income or intellectual property. It depends on the volume of new orders. And once the investment cycle begins to slow down, it will immediately reflect on revenue and profits.

An additional risk is that large customers can change the supply structure. Some of them are already trying to work directly with manufacturers or develop their own solutions. In such a scenario, the role of integrators such as Super Micro may gradually decrease.

Other Major Risks to Consider

After the latest depreciation of Super Micro's shares, it may seem that the bottom has been reached. But we do not agree with this. The market is still laying out a relatively optimistic scenario in which high growth rates persist and margins remain stable. In our model, which is presented later in this article, we use more conservative assumptions. We take into account the normalization of growth after peak demand, the subsequent pressure on margins, and the increased level of risk. We also use a more realistic cost of capital, which reflects the cyclical nature of the business. As such, we do not see a sufficient upside that would justify an investment in Super Micro's stock. On the contrary, the balance of risk and potential return remains negative.

The Real Value of Super Micro

To estimate Super Micro's intrinsic value, we made a valuation model when its stock was trading at $22.67 per share at the time of the analysis. The model assumes a constant growth rate of 2.0%, which is generally consistent with our long-term assumptions for tier-2 semiconductor companies. Our forecast covers the next five years and is based on data from the company's most recent earnings report, including cash balances and total debt, allowing us to assess the valuation sensitivity under more normalized growth conditions.

The model applies a discount rate of 9.45%, which we figured out after calculating Super Micro's cost of equity and cost of debt. The cost of debt was estimated primarily using trailing twelve-month data, while the cost of equity was calculated using the CAPM model, where the risk-free rate is 4.29%, a 1-year beta of 2.82, and a market return rate of 7.77%.

The sales forecast in the model assumes a decline in the growth rate, similar to our previous model. We also expect an operational margin of 5.5% in 2026 and a gradual decrease to 5.1% by 2030. The model also applies a 15% tax rate. Other financial assumptions remain mostly consistent with Super Micro's historical averages.

This model shows that Super Micro's enterprise value is $11.15 billion. After adding the cash balances and deducting the outstanding debt, the model showed Super Micro's equity value to be $9.99 billion, which translates to an intrinsic value of $16.68 per share. This represents a discount of approximately 28.14% from the current market price.

Risks to Our Bearish Thesis

Risks to our bearish thesis certainly exist. The biggest of them is that the AI boom can last longer than the market expects. If hyperscalers continue to invest aggressively in infrastructure for several more years, the demand for servers will remain high.

It also can't be ruled out that Super Micro might be able to improve its operational efficiency or move to more marginal products. If a company manages to establish itself as a greater partner within the AI infrastructure supply chain, then it could mitigate some risks described earlier in this article.

Final Thoughts

In the last year, the weaknesses of Super Micro's business have become more obvious. The company continues to have a high dependence on cyclical demand while at the same time has limited marginality and weak long-term revenue visibility. That is why we believe that after the latest depreciation, the current price still does not fully reflect all the risks. That is why we continue to rate Super Micro as a Sell.

data.title: Avalyn Pharma Begins IPO Process For Pulmonary Fibrosis Pipeline
data.description:
A Quick Take On Avalyn Pharma's IPO

Avalyn Pharma Inc. (AVLN) has filed to raise capital for its treatment pipeline development, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company is advancing drug candidate treatments for pulmonary fibrosis conditions.

AVLN has had encouraging tolerance and efficacy results in its lead candidate's trials.

The firm also has a strong investor syndicate and top-tier quality management in its favor.

Avalyn Overview and Development Efforts

Avalyn is a clinical-stage biopharma developing targeted treatment candidates for interstitial lung diseases [ILDs] like pulmonary fibrosis.

Pulmonary fibrosis results in the scarring of the lungs, leading to a decline in lung function and poor quality of life for suffering patients.

AVLN is developing inhaled formulations of already-approved drugs of pirfenidone and nintedanib for treating progressive pulmonary fibrosis and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

The current status of the company's drug candidate pipeline is shown here:

The firm's wholly owned lead candidate, AP01, is currently in Phase II trials and has 'demonstrated encouraging tolerability and clinical activity data…[with] low rates of side effects, including GI toxicities and liver enzyme elevations, which have been commonly seen with oral products. At 48 weeks, the high-dose AP01 achieved near stabilization of lung function, as measured by forced vital capacity, or FVC.' (Source: SEC)

Management is led by President and Chief Executive Officer Ms. Lyn Baranowski, who has been with the firm since October 2022 and was previously COO of Altavant, where she oversaw the development of therapies for rare respiratory diseases until the company's acquisition by Sumitomo Dainippon in 2019.

Avalyn's Market and Competitors

The global idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF] treatment market was an estimated $3.7 billion in 2024 and was expected to reach $5.5 billion by 2030, according to a market research report by Grand View Research.

If achieved, this growth would represent a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 6.8% from 2025 to 2030.

Since older individuals are more susceptible to developing chronic diseases like IPF, market demand is expected to grow as the global population continues to age, along with the increasing life expectancy of populations across the globe.

The chart below illustrates the historical and projected future growth expectations of the worldwide IPF treatment market by drug class through 2030:

In 2024, the North America region accounted for the highest market share, at 37.5%.

The Asia Pacific region is expected to produce the highest growth rate through 2030, at a CAGR of 7.8%.

By route of administration, orally administered medications had nearly 70% of the market in 2024.

Competitors with existing products or candidates in development include:

  • Legacy Pharma

  • Genentech/Roche (RHHBY)

  • Boehringer Ingelheim

  • United Therapeutics (UTHR)

  • Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY)

  • Insmed (INSM)

  • Celea Therapeutics

  • Vicore Pharma

  • Endeavor Biomedicines

  • Contineum Therapeutics (CTNM)

  • Eli Lilly and Co. (LLY)

  • Others

The company is also actively developing treatments for progressive pulmonary fibrosis, which is a smaller market size than idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, according to public research reports.

Avalyn's Financial Results

The firm's last two years of financial results are typical of a clinical-stage biopharma in that they feature significant R&D and G&A expenses associated with its pipeline development efforts and no revenue.

As of December 31, 2025, Avalyn had $138.4 million in cash, equivalent and marketable securities, and $15.3 million in total liabilities.

The company's investor syndicate had invested a fair market value total of $399 million in investment as of the end of 2025.

IPO Details

AVLN has filed to offer shares of its common stock and has used a placeholder figure of $100 million for purposes of the SEC filing review fee calculation, although the final amount may be higher.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period from the effective date of the prospectus, as determined by the SEC.

AVLN said it intends to utilize the IPO net proceeds as described below:

to advance development of AP01 through Phase 2b topline data and into Phase 3 development;

to advance development of AP02 through Phase 2 topline data and into Phase 3 development;

to advance development of AP03 through clinical development and Phase 1 topline data; and

the remainder for research and development activities for additional programs, working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source: SEC)

Management said the company is not currently a party to any legal proceedings that it believes would have a material adverse effect on its business, operations, or financial condition.

The listed underwriters of the IPO include Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, Evercore ISI, and Guggenheim Securities, although additional entities may be added later, at management's discretion.

My Thoughts About Avalyn's IPO

Avalyn is seeking public capital as it enters the heart of its clinical development, with two candidates in trials and one preparing to enter Phase 1 trials.

The company's lead candidate, a different formulation of an existing, approved drug, has produced positive tolerability and efficacy in trials to date, so the firm's outlook is positive in this regard.

The overall market for ILD treatments is large and expected to grow at a moderate rate in the coming years, although it features a number of major players and several potential new entrants, such as Avalyn, who are seeking to further develop treatment options for patients.

The company's investor syndicate includes a number of well-known life science venture capital firms and strategic investors, so its investor base is quite strong and a positive asset for the firm.

Avalyn is well-capitalized, and it will need to be further capitalized not only with the IPO's net proceeds but likely additional capital infusions afterward, as it is still relatively early in its pipeline development and entering the most cash-intensive part of its development lifecycle.

Management is also a strong suit for the company, with C-suite officers all having extensive life science industry experience in their respective employment histories.

As to risks, the biggest risk would be trial-related, whether not meeting trial endpoint expectations or having material adverse events among the trial patients.

The company is also using the 'smaller reporting company' and 'emerging growth company' designations, which allow management to produce less information for shareholders.

Many of these types of company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO, although they tend to be concentrated in general technology sectors and not in the life sciences.

Also, the biopharma sector has seen highly bifurcated performance post-IPO over the last few years, with some firms performing very well and others quite poorly, so the biopharma IPO market has been volatile and difficult to predict.

And reformulation of existing products may not produce a superior outcome with enough of a differentiation from the standard of care.

Management's pricing and valuation expectations will be important for prospective life science investors in the IPO.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined

data.title: XLF: Strong Buy For U.S. Financials During A Multi-Year Earnings Reset
data.description:
The State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF) seeks to provide investment results that correspond to the price and yield performance of the Financial Select Sector Index. XLF provides an investor exposure to the financial services industry, including banks, capital market firms, insurance, payment processors, and consumer finance companies. I am initiating XLF on Seeking Alpha as a strong buy, as this ETF offers great exposure to a sector that is repricing higher on three drivers at once. Between investment banking fee resurgence, Wells Fargo's first unconstrained year in seven, and a deregulation tailwind that consensus models are not yet pricing in, the financial sector is poised for very strong growth for the rest of the year.

XLF is down approx. 6.3% YTD. The setup for a strong buy is contrarian from non-institutional positioning, as the prime brokerage desk at Goldman Sachs has reported that hedge funds are aggressively shorting the sector. With earnings around the corner for the majority of the sector, any EPS deliveries short of a disaster would warrant a short cover worth a few percent.

Earnings

For most of 2024 and into 2025, the bear case on the financial sector was that net interest margin tailwinds were rolling over, and that fee income wouldn't be enough to compensate for this. This discussion is shrinking, as JPMorgan (JPM) has guided mid-teens YoY growth in IB fees in 2026. Additionally, the M&A market is ramping up. JPM additionally guided full-year 2026 NII to approx. $103 billion in total, alongside the Zacks consensus for Q1 NII at approx. 25.6B, which is a 10.1% increase YoY. While there is some discussion that NII is flattening, while technically true, it is misleading. The level it is flattening at is at record highs and not rolling over. Additionally, the spread economics within the curve remains favorable for GSIBs. The yield curve un-inverted in late 2025 into a classic bull steepening pattern, with the 10Y settling around 4.16% vs. the 2Y near 3.48%. Additionally, Angel Oak notes that many fixed-rate assets are expected to reprice higher as intermediate yields remain above levels at which those assets were originally put on bank balance sheets, which is a multi-quarter tailwind that will continue feeding financial strength.

The above matters because the index is less rate-sensitive than what the market believes. Goldman Sachs (GS) and Morgan Stanley (MS) can be viewed as pure plays on IB fee growth, while Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA) are pure plays on consumer transaction volume, which is keeping consumer spending resilient, and something like Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)/(BRK.B) is a long duration carry portfolio that is attached to operating businesses. Half of the funds weight goes into fee-based models that are positively levered to the M&A and capital markets cycles, instead of the rate cycle.

Wells Fargo Unleashed

One of the more underappreciated holdings within XLF is Wells Fargo (WFC). WFC is now able to grow its balance sheet, take on incremental loan volume, and redeploy trapped capital that was behind regulatory restrictions. After 7 years of operating under a $1.95 trillion Fed Reserve asset cap imposed in 2018, WFC was freed from this in June 2025, and on top of that, the Fed terminated the 2018 enforcement action in March of 2026, resolving all 14 consent orders that had been imposed on WFC at once. On WFC alone, the price appreciation investors should see over the next 1-2 years should flow through to drive further growth in XLF.

Deregulation

The third leg of the strong buy thesis is the regulatory environment. Basel III endgame implementation has been softened relative to the original 2023 proposal, the leverage ratio framework is under review, and the supplementary leverage ratio treatment of Treasuries is widely expected to be relaxed. Each of these is incrementally accretive to ROTCE at the mega cap banks, and each frees capital that gets returned through buybacks and dividends. Capital return acceleration into 2026 and 2027 is a very strong catalyst.

Inside the ETF

The top holdings of XLF are below:

Rank Ticker Company Sub-Industry Weight
1 BRK.B Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B Multi-line Insurance / Holding Co. 12.46%
2 JPM JPMorgan Chase & Co. Diversified Banks 11.24%
3 V Visa Inc. Class A Transaction Processing 7.20%
4 MA Mastercard Incorporated Class A Transaction Processing 5.77%
5 BAC Bank of America Corp. Diversified Banks 4.59%
6 GS Goldman Sachs Group Inc. Investment Banking & Brokerage 3.60%
7 WFC Wells Fargo & Co. Diversified Banks 3.49%
8 C Citigroup Inc. Diversified Banks 2.81%
9 MS Morgan Stanley Investment Banking & Brokerage 2.81%
10 AXP American Express Company Consumer Finance 2.30%

Top 10 Total 56.26%

The top 10 holdings of XLF total 56.26% of assets. There are 81 total names, including other large cap names spanning capital markets, payment processors, traditional banking, insurance, and consumer finance.

There are some additional positives to mention about XLF. XLF does exclude pure asset managers and most regional banks that are beneath the mega-cap tier, which is why I like XLF over an ETF such as KRE. I do not find it particularly attractive to own the regional bank tail, while commercial real estate continues to grind up losses. XLF allows an investor to sidestep that exposure in an effective way. Additionally, the expense ratio is 8 bps, so this ETF offers very cheap exposure to large-cap U.S. financials in a concentrated but semi-diversified portfolio.

Tradability

The average daily volume of XLF runs at approx. 50 million shares, with bid-ask spreads being around 1 cent during market hours. The options chain is very liquid for XLF as well. XLF is one of the only thematic ETFs where institutional positions can move in and out without impacting the market for the ETF. The size and scale of the fund keep the premium and discount to NAV flat as well. XLF additionally saw $1.2 billion of net inflows on a single day in late January 2026, with the fund continuing to attract interest, even with some bearish narratives being discussed in public news.

Downside Risk

With financials in general, and XLF, there is a fair downside risk to discuss. If the U.S. enters a hard landing recession, the sector betas to financials would be brutal, and credit deterioration would compound the directional move down. Something to watch for this would be the 2026 card net charge off rate. A meaningful jump past 3.4% would cause me to rethink the credit normalization assumption. Additionally, a private credit blowup bleeding into publicly traded financials is a tail risk that I am more concerned about, as redemption capping occurring at prominent firms (such as Blue Owl capping redemptions at 5% on two of its PC funds in early April after they were asked to pull 21.9% shares in Q1, in addition to permanently halting quarterly redemptions on OBDC II and switching to return of capital distributions) raises deep concerns about a largely unregulated credit environment, plagued by borrowers either utilizing PIK loans (with public BDCs now receiving an average of 7% in investment income via PIK loans) or failing to pay repayments and defaulting (with 40% of PC borrowers having negative FCF) is something that is growing into a larger issue that needs to be addressed. Lastly, global financial institutions are large targets for cyberattacks, and with geopolitical escalations, a cybersecurity event at a major bank (while low probability) would be severe.

Takeaway

The current base case for myself is that the Fed delivers at most one cut in 2026, with September or October the earliest realistic window. Hedge funds are aggressively shorting a sector with three structural tailwinds compounding at once: investment banking fee resurgence, Wells Fargo's first unconstrained year in seven, and a deregulation development that will fuel further growth. Due to the strength and potential growth of the sector, XLF earns a strong buy.

data.title: Intel: Why The Alphabet Collaboration Is A Bigger Signal Than It Looks
data.description:
Thesis

Intel Corporation (INTC) has just expanded its partnership with Alphabet/Google (GOOG). For me, it signals that Xeon CPUs are not likely to be displaced in the AI era, but rather they seem to be becoming more deeply embedded at the core of hyperscale infrastructure, as I’ll explain. It's a good trend for Intel, and the collaboration would imply that they’re evolving from a component supplier into a strategic systems partner. They’re set to co-design balanced architectures alongside custom IPUs, which should position them to benefit directly from scaling AI demand.

Elsewhere, the valuation would reflect a turnaround already being priced in. So in that sense, this deal would then reinforce the durability of CPU demand and support the long-term earnings recovery story here. I last covered Intel towards the end of last year, in which I explained what the Apple (AAPL) collaboration over the 18A node meant for investors. I see this collaboration as another step in the right direction for Intel's recovery and would remain Buy-rated again.

Intel and Google continue their collaboration

As you know, Intel and Google have just recently expanded their partnership. I see it as a pretty subtle but important shift, specifically in how AI infrastructure is evolving for both parties. You see, much of the recent AI boom has been focused on GPUs and specialized accelerators. And it is true, many companies have seen huge share momentum from expanding their GPU portfolio. However, this collaboration reinforces that CPUs are still the backbone of large-scale systems here.

On Intel’s side, it comes down to their Xeon processors. These processors basically orchestrate AI workflows in that they combine a very strong general-purpose compute with system-level control capabilities. This is what accelerators lack. Architecturally speaking, the Xeon can offer us much higher core counts and large shared caches. They also have wide vector engines, specifically the AVX-512 and the AMX for AI. Elsewhere, they run high memory bandwidth with DDR5, which is what allows them to very efficiently handle data preprocessing tasks like decoding, filtering, and batching. Now, these tasks are always in demand with the accelerating AI momentum, but they’re also usually operations that carry irregular logic and heavy memory access that GPUs struggle with.

So in that sense, they also act as the control plane of AI systems. And to bring this back to real-world deployments like Google Cloud, Xeons will essentially prepare and route data for large-scale training. They will also be particularly useful for latency-sensitive services that Google runs, like search/recommendations. They handle request processing and can even run smaller inference workloads using AMX, which should avoid unnecessary accelerator use. So overall, this results in better utilization of expensive hardware and lower latency. It sounds complicated, but if we just keep in mind that Google wants their offerings like Google Cloud, Search, YouTube, and its Gemini to be faster, more efficient, and a bit cheaper to run, we can see what they’re getting from this collaboration.

Advancing AI Infrastructure with Xeon

We also have to take into account here that Google is continuing to rely on Xeon across their cloud instances. They, of course, have the C4 and N4, which tells me that even as accelerators still dominate headlines, general-purpose compute is still extremely essential for balancing performance/latency and also cutting costs. What I mean by this is that we should take it as a signal that, despite the AI boom being centered on GPUs/TPUs, Intel’s Xeon CPUs are still very much embedded in the core of Google’s infrastructure here. There will be a stable demand for Xeon tied to every AI workload, not replaced by accelerators. For example, even when Google runs their Gemini models on TPUs, Xeon CPUs are still needed to preprocess the data and manage requests. They also have to coordinate that distributed training so as AI usage grows, CPU demand can scale alongside it rather than being displaced.

Elsewhere, we have the deepening co-development of custom ASIC-based IPUs to consider. It seems there’s a growing emphasis on offloading infrastructure-level tasks away from CPUs. So that means there is a push toward custom ASIC-based IPUs, and I see it as a shift in how hyperscale systems are being optimized in 2026. You see, instead of having CPUs handle everything, some specific infrastructure tasks, like networking, storage management, encryption, and, again, data movement, are being offloaded to dedicated chips designed for those exact functions. The whole environment, in this sense, is moving towards micromanagement and efficiency.

We know that CPUs are flexible, but they’re not actually the most efficient at these kinds of repetitive, high-throughput tasks. So hyperscalers would rather offload them to IPUs to reduce overhead and latency. In doing this, it also frees CPU cores, which can then focus on higher-value work like orchestration and AI pipeline management, as I explained earlier. Now, in theory, an IPU could handle packet processing, storage I/O, and security isolation independently. So this should improve consistency and utilisation across a massive data centre fleet.

I’m mentioning the specifics of this trend since it’s highly relevant to the Intel/Google collaboration here. Essentially, it tells me that they want to co-design a balanced system, and not just improve standalone chips. Xeon CPUs are pairing with custom IPUs here, so Google can scale AI infrastructure a lot more efficiently. While Xeons remain the control layer, the IPUs will take over the plumbing of the data center. For Intel, this is clearly good news, as per the 5% share price jump. It definitely deepens their role beyond just selling CPUs and more into a strategic partner role in system architecture. Google sees them as essential in reducing the total cost of ownership and improving performance at scale.

Valuation is the risk

As for a fair valuation, the stock does seem to trade at a premium on some multiples. I see that the market is pricing in a significant earnings recovery here. The forward price-to-earnings sits at about 125x for 2026, well above the sector median in the 22x range, and alongside a forward non-GAAP PEG of 4.49x. So this tells me that even with expected growth, it still looks expensive on a near-term earnings basis. Over the last year, or essentially since Lip Bu Tan joined Intel, the stock price has been up about 187%. It's heavily outperformed the S&P 500 (SPY), as you can see below.

Revenue-based multiples like EV to sales sit at 6.2x, and price-to-sales at 5.3x are also nearly double the sector medians, which is pretty notable here given Intel’s relatively modest revenue growth profile compared to their high-growth AI peers. However, these multiples do compress quite sharply over time, with the consensus estimates suggesting a drop to about 23x price to earnings by 2029. This would be driven by pretty aggressive EPS growth expectations, which include over 100% growth in 2027. So it would seem that the market is betting heavily on a successful turnaround here, specifically margin expansion, AI-driven demand, and much improved capital efficiency from their manufacturing/foundry strategy.

I find it hard to disagree with the market here in that sense. The current mid-$61 stock price does seem expensive considering the run-up in price over the last year, but in short, Intel is being valued less on what it is today and more on what it is expected to become over the next 3 to 5 years. That upside case would then justify the current level.

Looking ahead

Just to sum up, I would remain Buy-rated at current levels on Intel, since this partnership simply reinforces my core thesis here in that Xeon CPUs are not being displaced anytime soon in the AI era. Instead, I see them as becoming more deeply embedded within increasingly complex, hyperscale systems, which is good news for Intel. Intel is shifting more toward a strategic infrastructure partner, co-designing balanced architectures with IPUs rather than simply supplying components. This should help their long-term positioning in AI spending going forward. And yes, the valuation already reflects a pretty meaningful turnaround and leaves a lot less room for error in the near term, but the durability of CPU demand alongside accelerating AI workloads would make the case for a longer-term earnings recovery story.

data.title: March CPI: Gas Prices Surge The Most On Record, Real Wage Growth Drops
data.description:
CPI inflation surged in March, rising 0.865%, matching estimates, as an oil-driven energy shock led the way. It was good enough for a top-10 biggest sequential CPI hike since 1970. While headline inflation jumped, core CPI increased a more modest 0.196%, a tick under economists’ projections, with food prices flat.

Energy spiked 10.9% (the largest rise in 21 years), driven by a sharp 21% rise in gasoline (the biggest surge on record, back to 1967). Gasoline accounted for almost three-quarters of the inflation spike. On an annual basis, headline CPI is 3.3%, while the core rate is 2.6%.

Digging into the report, core goods rose just 0.1%, with new vehicles flat, used cars down 0.4%, and apparel up 1%. Core services also increased 0.2%, led by shelter (+0.3%), including rent (+0.2%) and OER (+0.3%), while hotels rose 0.2%. Medical care and car insurance were flat, though airfares climbed 2.7%. Big picture, super core CPI rose 18 basis points last month, which was an improvement from +35 basis points in February. Also keep in mind that the partial government shutdown may have shaved some pricing pressure.

For the consumer, real average hourly earnings are now a paltry +0.3% on a trailing 12-month basis, down sharply from 1.4% in February. Higher energy bills weigh on households, with electricity CPI up 4.6% over the past 12 months and gas utilities up 6.4% vs March 2025. Food-at-home CPI is +1.9%, but food away from home is hotter at +3.8%. The good news is that vehicle prices are up a weak 0.5% YoY, with used vehicle prices off 3.2%.

Equity futures were little changed after the 8:30 a.m. ET data, while the bond market largely yawned at the March numbers. The bulls are fine with that, as the S&P 500 (SP500) looks to put in a second consecutive large weekly climb. As for Fed Fund futures, there was a modest uptick in expectations for an eventual rate cut. Of course, the FOMC is likely to be in a holding pattern for quite some time.

The onus is now on the bulls to carry the S&P 500 to its 8th straight positive session.

March CPI Close to Consensus, Stocks Inch Higher

March CPI +0.9% as expected ... biggest jump since June 2022

CPI YoY now 3.3%, highest since April 2024

Core CPI up just 0.2%, below estimates

Core CPI YoY 2.6%, up MoM, but below estimates

Core goods CPI at +1.2% y/y in March vs. +1.0% prior. Core services +3.0% y/y vs. +2.9% prior

S&P 500 futures up a touch

Yields Little Changed

62% Implied Chance of at Least 1 Fed Rate Cut This Year

Looking ahead, more key data hits next week. UMich will be interesting today, given much-higher prices at the pump, and then Tuesday’s March PPI report offers a fresh look at wholesale inflation trends.

Before that, housing data hits Monday, with March Existing Home Sales, while NFIB Small Business Optimism on Tuesday morning could point toward continued optimism in hiring intentions from Main Street companies (recall that March ADP employment was quite strong among small- and medium-sized businesses).

Later, Empire Manufacturing for April, along with the Fed’s Beige Book, offers clues on the state of regional economic conditions. Jobless claims, Philly Fed, and Industrial Production round out the data week, with Friday’s calendar all clear. Bank earnings will create potential volatility in the Financials sector, too.

Data Deck

Bank earnings on tap

The Bottom Line

March CPI came in close to estimates, with the headline rate matching forecasts, while the core rate was a touch light. Stocks and bonds took the data in stride, with all eyes fixed on the conflict in Iran. Earnings season ramps up next week, too, with plenty of data catalysts on tap.

data.title: LyondellBasell: North America's Cost Advantage Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction

LyondellBasell (LYB) has been a great winner since I first started to cover the chemical firm which has appreciated a total of 36% since my first Buy rating and over 10% since my last review. LYB and its other peer Dow (DOW) have seen huge financial stress over the past couple of years and the tide seems to be finally turning. I’ll dive into why I believe the firm could continue to win.

Current Dynamics

The main bullish argument has been based on the conflict in the Middle East, as this instability has created immense volatility in the energy and feedstock markets that have put a priority on US-based chemical companies. See, as companies outside of North America produce ethylene and propylene, they rely on naphtha, in which the price is correlated with crude oil, that has been a real thorn for European and Asian manufacturers. On the other hand, LYB’s assets in North America are mainly powered by NGLs, mainly ethane, which is decoupled from crude oil prices. This essential divergence has created a massive margin windfall for the company’s US operations as the global price for PE and PP is set by the high-cost producers in the rest of the world.

Data shows that European ethylene prices reached $1.21 per kilogram due to these high energy price dynamics. Though on the NA side, prices remain significantly lower at 53 cents per kilogram, translating to pure margin capture for LYB, as for every $100 per metric ton increase in the price of PE, the company will generate around $320MM in additional annualized EBITDA. Secondly, while the firm’s European assets have faced higher costs, they have also been able to operate without Middle Eastern competition as exports have been trapped behind the Strait of Hormuz. These two dynamics have allowed LYB to implement aggressive price increases in Europe to protect margins with spot prices increasing by nearly 40%.

As LYB closed its Houston refinery, that was a significant source of earnings volatility, it is now pivoting the assets toward being the Houston Hub for Circular and Low Carbon Solutions. LYB will convert existing hydrotheaters and infrastructure for use in recycling technology. LYB is transforming a liability into a high-growth asset aligned with the future regulatory mandates. The hub will also include a massive plastic waste sorting facility and a second MoReTec advanced recycling plant, making LYB the dominant player in the circular economy in the US Gulf Coast. I find this approach relatively healthy for the firm as it is utilizing a foreclosed site that was already causing a lot of pain to the business and turning it into potentially one of the greatest green assets in North America.

Management also highlighted how polypropylene is a sleeping giant that is finally beginning to stir. PP margins have generally trailed PE due to oversupply and high propane feedsto costs, though the current environment has fundamentally changed this dynamic. Propane logistics has been heavily disrupted due to the situation in the Persian Gulf, which caused PDH operations in Asia and other high-cost regions to plummet to the mid-50% range. This global supply contraction in PP has coincided with a period of low domestic inventory, as US PE inventories were at just 37.7 days, well below the 45-day level typically required to balance the market. This tight supply and demand balance means that LYB will drive outsized earnings gains as pricing power returns to the PP segment.

On the European front, the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism, which is now active in 2026, will serve as a double-edged sword that will ultimately protect LYB’s existing assets in the region from low-cost and high-carbon imports. By effectively taxing imports based on their carbon intensity, CBAM effectively raised the cost for competitors who rely on older and less efficient technologies, thereby preserving LYB’s market share. In the region where it is investing heavily in low-carbon solutions.

Valuation

On simple financial metrics, it is hard to estimate LYB’s value. It has an EV/EBITDA of 16.20, which stands at the middle of the peer range. As free cash flow also got depressed and the stock shot up, the yield is currently slim at 1.6%. I also did a scenario analysis for the company that, in a bull case, which I believe is currently in the cards considering the bullish dynamics, it should push the stock to an implied price of $95.34 per share. Though a source of skepticism has been S&P Global lowering the credit rating to BBB-, due to earnings and credit metrics that fell below previous expectations as the cyclical trough continued for longer. Though S&P expects EBITDA to improve to a range of $3.3B to $4.3B through 2028 as cost-saving initiatives take hold. LYB is also trading at a forward yield of 3.72%, one of the lowest levels since 2022.

One of the most critical growth prospects for the firm would be the Value Enhancement Program, which focuses on modernization and AI-driven predictive maintenance. The program delivered $1.1B in recurring annual EBITDA, plus management is expecting to extend that target to $1.5B in recurring annual EBITDA by 2028. LYB also needs to continue to showcase a high cash conversion rate to reflect the resilience of the LYB operating model. LYB achieved an exceptional 95% cash conversion rate and the firm is now targeting a long-term average of 80%. This high conversion allows the company to maintain its current dividend yield while simultaneously throwing billions into its CapEx program for the pivot.

Risks

The recent stock performance has been driven by the war premium created by the conflict in the Middle East, but if this rapidly de-escalates or the Strait of Hormuz fully opens, the temporary 10% tightening of global polyethylene supply would normalize, causing LYB to lose its North American cost advantage.

A massive wave of new production capacity coming mainly from China continues to depress global margins for polyethylene. S&P global notes that significant capacity is still slated to come online through 2027, which could effectively prolong this trough.

Conclusion

LyondellBasell has been surfing on a perfect storm after going through a tumultuous set of years that have hampered the financial footing of the firm. Though I’ll say that refining business exit as well as the dividend pivot have signaled that management is unafraid to make the hard decisions to preserve long-term value. I believe that the stock can go higher outside of the conflict in the Middle East. In that sense, I keep my Buy rating on LyondellBasell.

data.title: Gatekeeper Systems: Traders Losing Patience During Long Radio Silence
data.description:
I have been covering Gatekeeper Systems (GSI:CA) (GKPRF) on SeekingAlpha since summer 2024, when I first issued a bullish call on the stock in light of Gatekeeper's strong position in what was about to become a big market: upgrading passenger train locomotives with crash-hardened memory modules, as required by a Federal Railroad Administration [FRA] mandate. Basically, the federal government ruled that all locomotives of passenger trains must have security cameras by October, 2027. This should be easy to understand: planes have "black boxes" to help with post-mortem analysis after a crash, and this legislation just requires the same thing for trains. Since there are relatively few companies actively engaged in this market, and Gatekeeper has already installed these units for trains in Philadelphia's SEPTA system, they have been poised to capture an outsized portion of this ~$300m (according to the company) opportunity:

Shares went up about 600% last year as the first deals started to get announced, and Gatekeeper won a big one: Long Island Railroad [LIRR], for $27m CAD. Shares broke $2 USD last fall in the aftermath of that deal. Shortly after the results were announced, I sized up Gatekeeper's prospects in another article, urging readers to stay cautious, since:

the LIRR contract is just one data point, and we really still do not know how many more contracts Gatekeeper will win. There is very little visibility here, and Gatekeeper's management has not provided any form of clear guidance.

2026 News: Not Much So Far!

In the five months since that article appeared, shares have fallen by about half. According to my internet research, no other company has announced winning a contract for FRA security-camera / memory-module installations in a major metropolitan rail system. But then again - Gatekeeper has not had any major wins, either. This in itself is not a reason to buy or sell shares; the absence of news says nothing definitive about future prospects.

A second "update" was a $13.5m private placement, which closed November 13 and was followed by a ~12% sell-off in shares. The offering priced shares at about $1.50 USD, and so shares sold off to that level and then traded around it until about New Years.

The FY2026 Q1 results, released on January 29th, were also unexciting. These results disappointed some investors but told us nothing important about the key questions: (1) how many more FRA deals Gatekeeper will win, and (2) whether those deals will be followed by SaaS subscriptions.

In the last few months, the company has issued two press releases announcing about $1.8m CAD in new contract wins for school bus camera systems. These contracts are not really enough to move the needle. It is nice to see, however, that several of them included SaaS subscriptions.

I am more excited to see continued updates about Gatekeeper's OEM integrations. First came announcement of a proof-of-concept with L&T Technology Services " a global leader in AI, Digital & ER&D Consulting Services, for design and delivery of video solutions for freight rail" for a contract in the Middle East. Then, on March 5 came another announcement about an OEM integration with Coach & Equipment Bus Sales Inc.

FRA Mandate Opportunity

It is worth reiterating that in the LIRR bidding process, Gatekeeper only competed against two other potential providers (Railhead and Wi-Tronix). Gatekeeper offered a price about 40% below railhead and only marginally below Wi-Tronix. In other words, the only "real" competitor in this contract race was Wi-Tronix.

Wi-Tronix is private, so there is relatively little information available about them online, but according to the company website, Wi-Tronix already partners with Amtrak and several major rail providers. Here is some background:

  • 2017: receives strategic investment from Siemens.
  • September 2023: won a $1.6m grant from the FRA to install cameras on the Brightline/FEC transit corridor.
  • January 2024: won a pilot competition to partner with Metro-North and New Jersey Transit.
  • June 2024: announced a partnership with Siemens (SIEGY) Mobility to implement their "integrated data and communication system" across the LA Metro. It is somewhat unclear exactly what all this contract entailed, and its focus seemed to be more on "real-time train information" than on "safety and security measures" (which were also mentioned), but we might well imagine that LA will use Wi-Tronix for their FRA compliance upgrades since they are already using them for this.
  • October 2024: won a $20.5m USD FRA grant to upgrade data connectivity on short-line railroads.

Today, they aren't hiring, but I think this track record speaks to the fact that Wi-Tronix will be a very significant competitor for the remaining FRA contracts.

We will probably get another data point on this competition in the next few months, because the South Florida Regional Transportation Authority [SFRTA] currently has a RFP posted with a response deadline of May 7.

So if we conservatively estimate that the real FRA opportunity is something like $270m (about 10% less than what Gatekeeper management claims), and ~$20m of that has already been awarded to Gatekeeper for the LIRR, then there is about $250m remaining to be awarded.

Valuation and Recommendation

Using these figures, I model Gatekeeper winning *very roughly* 20-40% of the $250m in outstanding FRA contracts, for a total of about $50-$100m USD. The contract awards will likely be spread out over the next ~18 months. And then, judging by the LIRR revenue recognition timeline, arrive in stages over the following 1-3 years. This means that the full expected revenue of ~$75 million (the midpoint of $50m-$100m) will be spread out over the next ~4-5 years for an annual contribution of ~$10-$20m, ramping up over the next 12 months before peaking in FY28 (ending in Aug. 2028). (Specifically, I'm modelling very little revenue arriving in FY26, ~$10-$15m arriving in FY27, ~$30-$50m arriving in FY28, and then the remainder arriving in FY29-FY30.) Adding that on to Gatekeeper's current ~$30m revenue run rate, we come to a range of FY28 revenue expectations around $50-$70 million. Assuming roughly flat gross margins (which seems conservative given that Gatekeeper had to compete on price for the LIRR contract) and a modest amount of operating leverage, we get to a FY28 EPS of $0.05-$0.10 USD in EPS.

If we land at the high end of that range and many of Gatekeeper's FRA contracts lead to longer-running SaaS contracts, shares will appreciate considerably due to (1) higher SaaS margins and (2) the establishment of a stronger base of recurring revenue. But given that we are already almost halfway through FY26, it seems unlikely that much additional FRA revenue lands in this fiscal year. And it will take time for the contracts to be won and delivery to begin in FY27. So realistically, at current prices ($0.92 USD as of this writing), you are paying ~12x FY2028 earnings. For a company where most revenue is still coming from relatively low-margin equipment sales, and probably at least a year from posting solid profits, I don't think this is a crazy valuation. It prices in, at least in part, the significant remaining uncertainty about future FRA contract wins, the timing of revenue recognition, and the conversion of those wins to recurring SaaS revenue. I think Gatekeeper remains a "show-me" story.

FY2028 Bear Base Bull
EPS $ 0.05 $ 0.08 $ 0.10
P/E 15 (hardware) $ 0.75 $ 1.13 $ 1.50
P/E 20 (software) $ 1.00 $ 1.50 $ 2.00

SeekingAlpha categorizes Gatekeeper as an IT company, but in practice most of their revenue still comes from relatively low-margin equipment sales, and so I think we should be conservative about expecting the market to pay a high multiple for what could very well be unsustainable earnings growth.

In terms of risks, I am not worried about dilution. Gatekeeper raised ~$20m USD last year through two rounds of financing. In the latest quarterly reported (from January 29), the company reported $18m CAD in cash, $17m CAD in other additional working capital, and $13.5m CAD in two undrawn credit facilities. Given that the company is near-breakeven, and operating cash flow has been positive, on balance, in the decade since the company went public, I don't see any need for additional financing unless the company wins even more large contracts, which would on balance be good for shareholders.

Looking at the company's history, shares have compounded at almost a 30% CAGR in the decade since the IPO. But that long-term trend has felt very different for investors, who have seen shares swing widely, routinely doubling or tripling before losing half or even four-fifths of their value in huge multi-year swings. We have now entered another downtrend. I won't pretend to know what will happen next, but I think the long-term trend is a sober reminder that shares could fall much further as investors get sick of waiting for big new contract wins.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
